title: Sci-Fi Writer Arthur C. Clarke Predicts the Future in 1964: Artificial Intelligence, Instantaneous Global Communication, Remote Work, Singularity & More
description:
in Science, Technology |   	April 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Are you feel┬şing con┬şfi┬şdent about the future? No? We under┬şstand. Would you
body:
in Science, Technology |   	April 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Are you feel┬şing con┬şfi┬şdent about the future? No? We under┬şstand. Would you like to know what it was like to feel a deep cer┬ştain┬şty that the decades to come were going to be filled with won┬şder and the fan┬ştas┬ştic? Well then, gaze upon this clip from the BBC Archive YouTube chan┬şnel of sci-fi author Arthur C. Clarke pre┬şdict┬şing the future in 1964.
Although we best know him for writ┬şing 2001: A Space Odyssey, the 1964 tele┬şvi┬şsion-view┬şing pub┬şlic would have known him for his futur┬şism and his tal┬şent for calm┬şly explain┬şing all the great things to come. In the late 1940s, he had already pre┬şdict┬şed telecom┬şmu┬şni┬şca┬ştion satel┬şlites. In 1962 he pub┬şlished his col┬şlect┬şed essays, Pro┬şfiles of the Future, which con┬ştains many of the ideas in this clip.
Here he cor┬şrect┬şly pre┬şdicts the ease with which we can be con┬ştact┬şed wher┬şev┬şer in the world we choose to, where we can con┬ştact our friends ÔÇťany┬şwhere on earth even if we donÔÇÖt know their loca┬ştion.ÔÇŁ What Clarke doesnÔÇÖt pre┬şdict here is how ÔÇťloca┬ştionÔÇŁ isnÔÇÖt a thing when weÔÇÖre on the inter┬şnet. He imag┬şines peo┬şple work┬şing just as well from Tahi┬şti or Bali as they do from Lon┬şdon. Clarke sees this advance┬şment as the down┬şfall of the mod┬şern city, as we do not need to com┬şmute into the city to work. Now, as so many of us are doing our jobs from home post-COVID, weÔÇÖve also dis┬şcov┬şered the dystopia in that fan┬şta┬şsy. (It cer┬ştain┬şly has┬şnÔÇÖt dropped the cost of rent.)
Next, he pre┬şdicts advances in biotech┬şnol┬şo┬şgy that would allow us to, say, train mon┬şkeys to work as ser┬şvants and work┬şers. (Until, he jokes, they form a union and ÔÇťweÔÇÖd be back right where we start┬şed.) Per┬şhaps, he says, humans have stopped evolvingÔÇöwhat comes next is arti┬şfi┬şcial intel┬şli┬şgence (although that phrase had yet to be used) and machine evo┬şlu┬ştion, where weÔÇÖd be hon┬şored to be the ÔÇťstep┬şping stoneÔÇŁ towards that des┬ştiny. Make of that what you will. I know you might think it would be cool to have a mon┬şkey but┬şler, but cÔÇÖmon, think of the ethics, not to men┬ştion the cost of bananas.
Point┬şing out where Clarke gets it wrong is too easyÔÇönobody gets it right all of the time. How┬şev┬şer, it is fas┬şci┬şnat┬şing that some things that have nev┬şer come to passÔÇöbeing able to learn a lan┬şguage overnight, or eras┬şing your memoriesÔÇöhave man┬şaged to resur┬şface over the years as sci┬şence fic┬ştion films, like Eter┬şnal Sun┬şshine of the Spot┬şless Mind. His ideas of cryo┬şgenic sus┬şpen┬şsion are sta┬şples of numer┬şous hard sci-fi films.
And we are still wait┬şing for the ÔÇťRepli┬şca┬ştorÔÇŁ machine, which would make exact dupli┬şcates of objects (and by so doing cause a col┬şlapse into ÔÇťglut┬şto┬şnous bar┬şbarismÔÇŁ because weÔÇÖd want unlim┬şit┬şed amounts of every┬şthing.) Some com┬şmenters call this a pre┬şcur┬şsor to 3ÔÇĹD print┬şing. IÔÇÖd say oth┬şer┬şwise, but some┬şthing very close to it might be around the cor┬şner. Who knows? Clarke him┬şself agrees about all this conjectureÔÇöitÔÇÖs doomed to fail.
ÔÇťThat is why the future is so end┬şless┬şly fas┬şci┬şnat┬şing. Try as we can, weÔÇÖll nev┬şer out┬şguess it.ÔÇŁ
Note: An ear┬şli┬şer ver┬şsion of this post appeared on our site in 2022.
Relat┬şed Con┬ştent:
How French Artists in 1899 Envi┬şsioned What Life Would Look Like in the Year 2000┬á
Isaac Asi┬şmov Pre┬şdicts the Future on┬áThe David Let┬şter┬şman Show┬á(1980)

In 1922, a Nov┬şel┬şist Pre┬şdicts What the World Will Look Like in 2022: Wire┬şless Tele┬şphones, 8ÔÇĹHour Flights to Europe & More
In 1894, A French Writer Pre┬şdict┬şed the End of Books & the Rise of Portable Audio┬şbooks and Pod┬şcasts
Ted Mills is a free┬şlance writer on the arts.

title: Isaac Asimov Reviews George OrwellÔÇÖs Nineteen Eighty-Four and Calls It ÔÇťNot Science Fiction, But a Distorted Nostalgia for a Past that Never WasÔÇŁ
description: Here in the twen┬şty-twen┬şties,┬áa young read┬şer first hear┬şing of George OrwellÔÇÖs Nine┬şteen Eighty-Four would hard┬şly imag┬şine it to be a work of
body:
Here in the twen┬şty-twen┬şties,┬áa young read┬şer first hear┬şing of George OrwellÔÇÖs Nine┬şteen Eighty-Four would hard┬şly imag┬şine it to be a work of sci┬şence fic┬ştion. That would┬şnÔÇÖt have been the case in 1949, when the nov┬şel was first pub┬şlished, and when the epony┬şmous year would have sound┬şed like the dis┬ştant future. Even as the actu┬şal nine┬şteen-eight┬şies came around, it still evoked visions of a tech┬şno-total┬şi┬ştar┬şi┬şan dystopia ahead. ÔÇťSo thor┬şough┬şly has 1984-opho┬şbia pen┬şe┬ştrat┬şed the con┬şscious┬şness of many who have not read the book and have no notion of what it con┬ştains, that one won┬şders what will hap┬şpen to us after 31 Decem┬şber 1984,ÔÇŁ wrote Isaac Asi┬şmov in 1980. ÔÇťWhen New YearÔÇÖs Day of 1985 arrives and the Unit┬şed States is still in exis┬ştence and fac┬şing very much the prob┬şlems it faces today, how will we express our fears of what┬şev┬şer aspect of life fills us with appre┬şhen┬şsion?ÔÇŁ
The occa┬şsion was one of a series of syn┬şdi┬şcat┬şed news┬şpa┬şper columns that Asi┬şmov seems to have pub┬şlished each new year. At the dawn of Nine┬şteen Eighty-FourÔÇÖs decade, the syn┬şdi┬şcate asked him to revis┬şit OrwellÔÇÖs nov┬şel, which had already been a com┬şmon cul┬ştur┬şal ref┬şer┬şence for decades. As a work of sci┬şence fic┬ştion (the genre for which his own name had prac┬şti┬şcal┬şly come to stand), he finds it lack┬şing, to say the least. ÔÇťThe Lon┬şdon in which the sto┬şry is placed is not so much moved thir┬şty-five years for┬şward in time, from 1949 to 1984, as it is moved a thou┬şsand miles east in space to Moscow,ÔÇŁ he writes. Far from attempt┬şing to imag┬şine the future, in Asi┬şmovÔÇÖs view, Orwell sim┬şply con┬şvert┬şed the Eng┬şland he knew into a drea┬şry Stal┬şin┬şist-type state. Apart from cer┬ştain implau┬şsi┬şble sur┬şveil┬şlance sys┬ştems, the set┬şting is ÔÇťincred┬şi┬şbly old-fash┬şioned when com┬şpared with the real world of the 1980s.ÔÇŁ
Orwell does┬şnÔÇÖt even both┬şer to imag┬şine any new vices: ÔÇťHis char┬şac┬şters are all gin hounds and tobac┬şco addicts,ÔÇŁ Asi┬şmov writes, ÔÇťand part of the hor┬şror of his pic┬şture of 1984 is his elo┬şquent descrip┬ştion of the low qual┬şi┬şty of the gin and tobac┬şco.ÔÇŁ That telling detail hints at one of OrwellÔÇÖs major sources of inspi┬şra┬ştion: the British Min┬şistry of Infor┬şma┬ştion, his wifeÔÇÖs employ┬şer dur┬şing World War II, and the source of the mate┬şr┬şi┬şal he broad┬şcast to India while work┬şing at the BBC around the same time.┬á The Min┬şistryÔÇÖs can┬şteen, accord┬şing to his let┬şters, was not of the high┬şest stan┬şdard. WhatÔÇÖs more, the 850-word ÔÇťBasic Eng┬şlishÔÇŁ that it insist┬şed on using in its broad┬şcasts bears more than a pass┬şing resem┬şblance to Nine┬şteen Eight-FourÔÇÖs Newspeak, the pared-down lan┬şguage devel┬şoped and man┬şdat┬şed by the gov┬şern┬şment in order to lim┬şit its cit┬şi┬şzensÔÇÖ range of thought.
Asi┬şmov does┬şnÔÇÖt buy that either. ÔÇťThere is no sign that such com┬şpres┬şsions of the lan┬şguage have ever weak┬şened it as a mode of expres┬şsion,ÔÇŁ he writes. ÔÇťAs a mat┬şter of fact, polit┬şi┬şcal obfus┬şca┬ştion has tend┬şed to use many words rather than few, long words rather than short, to extend rather than to reduce.ÔÇŁ (This, of course, was some┬şthing Orwell knew.) What┬şev┬şer Nine┬şteen Eighty-FourÔÇÖs short┬şcom┬şings as prophe┬şcy, sci-fi, or indeed lit┬şer┬şa┬şture, Asi┬şmov does cred┬şit Orwell with a cer┬ştain geopo┬şlit┬şi┬şcal savvy. Its world-rul┬şing trio of Ocea┬şnia, Eura┬şsia, and Eas┬şt┬şa┬şsia ÔÇťfits in, very rough┬şly, with the three actu┬şal super┬şpow┬şers of the 1980s: the Unit┬şed States, the Sovi┬şet Union, and Chi┬şna.ÔÇŁ Orwell knew, as many did┬şnÔÇÖt, that the lat┬şter two would not join forces, per┬şhaps thanks to his own frus┬ştrat┬şing expe┬şri┬şence fight┬şing for fac┬ştion┬şal┬şism-prone left caus┬şes. But not even as future-ori┬şent┬şed a mind as Asi┬şmovÔÇÖs would have guessed that, just a few years lat┬şer, the USSR would be out of the game ÔÇö and a few decades lat┬şer, the word Orwellian┬áwould be applied most often to Chi┬şna.
Read Asi┬şmovÔÇÖs take on 1984 here.
If you would like to sup┬şport the mis┬şsion of Open Cul┬şture, con┬şsid┬şer mak┬şing┬áa dona┬ştion to our site. ItÔÇÖs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬ştri┬şbu┬ştions will help us con┬ştin┬şue pro┬şvid┬şing the best free cul┬ştur┬şal and edu┬şca┬ştion┬şal mate┬şri┬şals to learn┬şers every┬şwhere. You can con┬ştribute through Pay┬şPal, Patre┬şon, and Ven┬şmo (@openculture). Thanks!
Relat┬şed con┬ştent:

Rid┬şley Scott on the Mak┬şing of AppleÔÇÖs Icon┬şic ÔÇť1984ÔÇŁ Com┬şmer┬şcial, Aired on Super Bowl Sun┬şday in 1984
Based in Seoul,┬áCol┬şin┬áMarshall┬áwrites and broad┬şcasts on cities, lan┬şguage, and cul┬şture. HeÔÇÖs the author of the newslet┬şter┬áBooks on Cities┬áas well as the books┬áÝĽťŕÁş ýÜöýĽŻ ŕŞłýžÇ┬á(No Sum┬şma┬şriz┬şing Korea) and┬áKore┬şan Newtro.┬áFol┬şlow him on the social net┬şwork for┬şmer┬şly known as Twit┬şter at┬á@colinmarshall.

title: Cinga Samson Conjures Mystery and the Sublime in Large-Scale Oil Paintings
description: Amid groves of trees, meadows, and aging infrastructure, Cinga SamsonÔÇÖs dreamlike tableaux are bathed in eerie light, as if spotlit or illuminated
body:
Amid groves of trees, meadows, and aging infrastructure, Cinga SamsonÔÇÖs dreamlike tableaux are bathed in eerie light, as if spotlit or illuminated by the moon. The South African artist is known for his use of deep, dark pigments such as carbon black and Prussian blue, complemented by the occasional teal or purple and pops of bright white in t-shirts or sneakers. His figures, engaged in enigmatic activities, look on with spectral, all-white eyes. Green and brown foliage camouflages individuals who gather in fields, sort through mysterious items, and appear to converge with other beings like large birds.
The work seen here is currently on view in the artistÔÇÖs solo exhibition at White Cube called Ukuphuthelwa. In the artistÔÇÖs native language, isiXhosa, the title means ÔÇťunable to sleep.ÔÇŁ ÔÇťUnlike the English word ÔÇśinsomnia,ÔÇÖ the isiXhosa term carries no negative connotation, and accordingly, for Samson, sleeplessness is not a condition to be cured but a state of spiritual alertness, a sensitivity that deepens in the dark,ÔÇŁ the gallery says. 
Detail of ÔÇťUkuwelwa komdaÔÇŁ
The nature of consciousness and even the realm of dreams is an apt entry point to SamsonÔÇÖs latest work, which examines how painting can be what he describes as ÔÇťtrue and honest.ÔÇŁ What is real? What do we imagine? Throughout art history, animals have long symbolized certain qualities or beliefs, such as dogs as allegories of fidelity and birds as messengers between the earthly and the divine. In the artistÔÇÖs large-scale compositions, there is a sense of ÔÇťreadabilityÔÇŁ akin to the narrative-driven Western art genre of history paintings, where the presence of these beings hints at meaning. Yet as we spend time with SamsonÔÇÖs works curiosity and wonder seem to always overtake glimpses of recognition.
Even though the artistÔÇÖs scenes may seem at first indecipherable, there is a foundational sense of reverence and ceremony in how his figures commune in seemingly out-of-the-way places for undisclosed, perhaps ritualistic purposes. Samson invites us to encounter the unknown, weaving a tension between what is knowable and what is unspecified or concealed. Through this, he explores the limits of representation in art and aims for what the gallery describes as ÔÇťthe authority of the unnameable and the territory of the sublime,ÔÇŁ where sacredness is present in holy spaces and everyday experiences alike.
Ukuphuthelwa continues through April 18 in New York City. See more on the artistÔÇÖs Instagram.
ÔÇťIyafunwaÔÇŁ (2026), oil on canvas, 86 1/2 x 103 3/8 inches
Detail of ÔÇťIyafunwaÔÇŁ
ÔÇťUmlindoÔÇŁ (2026), oil on canvas, 91 3/4 x 138 inches
Detail of ÔÇťUmlindoÔÇŁ
ÔÇťImfihloÔÇŁ (2026), oil on canvas, 55 x 110 1/2 inches
Detail ÔÇťImfihloÔÇŁ
ÔÇťIsiganekoÔÇŁ (2026), oil on canvas, 86 1/2 x 102 1/2 inches
Detail of ÔÇťIsiganekoÔÇŁ

title: Trump Plays Christ and Gets the Wound Wrong
description:
Opinion
                The presidentÔÇÖs latest attempt to fashion himself as an American messiah is costing him some of his most
body:
Opinion
                The presidentÔÇÖs latest attempt to fashion himself as an American messiah is costing him some of his most loyal Catholic supporters.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Trump's now-deleted Truth Social post contained an AI-generated image of him as Jesus (screenshot Hyperallergic)Yesterday, on Orthodox Easter, Donald Trump faced an eruption of backlash for posting an AI-generated photo of himself as Jesus Christ, just a couple of minutes after he criticized Pope Leo XIV on Truth Social. The image parodies the centuries-old Catholic image of the Divine Physician, with Trump wearing a flowing white mantle with a red robe, a beam of light emanating from his left hand as he rests his right on the forehead of a man in a hospital bed. Surrounding him are an older man in a dark navy cap (presumably a veteran), a man in military camouflage, a woman in a blue surgeon cap with a stethoscope, and a woman with hands folded in prayer ÔÇö all of whom are White. Behind him waves a rippling American flag, while eagles and fighter jets soar through the sky above the Statue of Liberty and the Lincoln Memorial.Trump posted the image right after going on a political tirade against Pope Leo about an hour after 60 Minutes aired a segment about the pope and American Catholic leadersÔÇÖ ongoing opposition against the presidentÔÇÖs war in Iran and immigration policies. In his post, Trump claims, ÔÇťIf I wasnÔÇÖt in the White House, Leo wouldnÔÇÖt be in the Vatican.ÔÇŁThe post was deleted after outcry from his loyalist supporters, including Bishop Robert Barron, who serves on the presidential administrationÔÇÖs Religious Liberty Commission, formed last May. Conservative pundit Michael Knowles ÔÇö himself a practicing Catholic ÔÇö posted that ÔÇťit behooves the President both spiritually and politically to delete the picture, no matter the intent.ÔÇŁ Even the Knights Templar International, a far-right Catholic group that has long supported Trump, said they ÔÇťdemand that this offensive and blasphemous image be removed forthwith.ÔÇŁ Today, Trump denied that the AI image depicted him as Jesus, claiming that it rendered him instead as a doctor.Last May, I wrote about how Trump posted an AI-generated meme of himself as the pope as a way of virtue signaling to MAGA Catholics. However, his latest attempt to fashion himself as an American messiah is costing him some of his most loyal Catholic supporters, who are now faced with the choice of publicly supporting the pope or the president. ItÔÇÖs not the first time that Catholics have had to choose.Despite the recent ascendency of wealthy White Catholics into positions of power, anti-Catholic sentiment is deeply rooted in American history. For much of the nationÔÇÖs lifespan, Catholics were targeted and discriminated against due to anti-Catholic, xenophobic bigotry. In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, as Catholic immigrants from Southern and Eastern Europe arrived in the United States, they faced intense nativist violence, including from the Ku Klux Klan. Now, the Trump administration is wielding nativist violence against immigrants and communities of color ÔÇö including Catholic Latinos ÔÇö whom he accuses of taking resources away from ÔÇťtrueÔÇŁ Americans: White Protestants.TrumpÔÇÖs absurdist image of himself as Jesus with a fake tan makes clear who he has come to ÔÇťsave.ÔÇŁ Just like 19th- and 20th-century anti-Catholic campaigns, Trump fashions himself a Messiah for American Protestant Christian nationalists, whom he blesses with a blazing, white light emanating from his left hand.But art historians and cradle Catholics alike will recognize that light is emanating out of the wrong body part ÔÇö light poured out of JesusÔÇÖs side wound, not his hand. ItÔÇÖs a perfect illustration of just how little Trump knows about the art and religion he is weaponizing, all in the name of his false White Christian Jesus.
                  
                      
                        Opinion
                      
                      
                        Donald Trump

schema.headline: Trump Plays Christ and Gets the Wound Wrong

title: Gagosian to Debut New Gallery With DuchampÔÇÖs ÔÇťReadymadesÔÇŁ
description:
News
                The exhibition will run concurrently with the artist's first United States retrospective in over 50 years a
body:
News
                The exhibition will run concurrently with the artist's first United States retrospective in over 50 years at the Museum of Modern Art.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Marcel Duchamp inside the exhibition The Art of Assemblage at the Museum of Modern Art, New York, 1961, with his artworks ÔÇťFountainÔÇŁ (1950, replica of lost 1917 original) and ÔÇťBicycle WheelÔÇŁ (1951, replica of lost 1913 original) (┬ę Association Marcel Duchamp/ADAGP, Paris/ Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York 2026; courtesy Gagosian, photo Marvin Lazarus)Remember brat summer? Well, apparently New York City is determined to have a Duchamp spring as Gagosian gallery announced that the inaugural exhibition at its new Uptown location will center the conceptual artist's famous ÔÇťreadymadesÔÇŁ ÔÇö including a replica series first exhibited at the same location over six decades ago. ┬áOpening April 25 and running concurrently with the artist's first United States retrospective in over 50 years at the Museum of Modern Art, Gagosian's forthcoming exhibition will present several of Duchamp's most recognized readymades ÔÇö pre-existing, mass-produced objects that the artist designated as artworks.The show is a tale of new beginnings at the same place for both the mega-gallery and the artist. Having opened its first NYC location at 980 Madison Avenue in 1989, Gagosian was recently forced to vacate its multi-level headquarters after Bloomberg Philanthropies bought out the building in 2024. Nevertheless, art dealer Larry Gagosian was determined to stay in the NYC flagship location of 35 years, and managed to secure a ground-level space at 980 Madison Avenue for the gallery.Installation view of Marcel Duchamp at the Museum of Modern Art (photo Lisa Yin Zhang/Hyperallergic)In 1964, Duchamp and his dealer, Arturo Schwarz, commemorated the 50th anniversary of the artist's first readymade ÔÇö ÔÇťBicycle WheelÔÇŁ (1913) ÔÇö by creating a limited edition set of 14 authorized replicas of original works that had been lost over time. The replica set made its American debut in a 1965 exhibition at Cordier & Ekstrom Gallery, then housed in the same Upper East Side building.(As it turns out, Cordier & Ekstrom Gallery also lost its lease at 980 Madison Avenue after 20 years in 1979 ... Time is a flat circle ÔÇö nay, a spinning bike wheel.)Since their inception, Duchamp's readymades have maintained a polarizing influence on modern and contemporary art, provoking conversations about what the term ÔÇťartÔÇŁ encompasses, and who gets to determine that, for over a century. It was ÔÇťFountainÔÇŁ (1917), an overturned porcelain urinal that the artist signed off with the pseudonym R. Mutt, that catapulted the body of work into fiery debate after Duchamp submitted it for display in an inaugural un-juried exhibition hosted by the Society of Independent Artists in NYC.Marcel Duchamp, ÔÇťL.H.O.O.Q.ÔÇŁ (1964, after the 1919 original) (┬ę Association Marcel Duchamp/ADAGP, Paris/ Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York 2026; courtesy Gagosian, photo Owen Conwa)The work's ultimate exclusion from the main show, which aimed to display all artists' submissions so long as they paid the required fee, led to Duchamp's resignation from the Society. The dialogue about the work's value and artistic designation continued in the following issue of the Blind Man, a short-lived Dadaist journal, which included a segment in defense of ÔÇťFountain.ÔÇŁAlongside replicas of ÔÇťFountainÔÇŁ and ÔÇŁBicycle Wheel,ÔÇŁ GagosianÔÇÖs forthcoming exhibition will also include Duchamp's ÔÇťL.H.O.O.Q.ÔÇŁ (1964, after 1919 original); ÔÇťPorte-bouteilles (Bottle Dryer)ÔÇŁ (1964, after 1914 lost original); and ÔÇťBo├«te-en-valiseÔÇŁ (1935ÔÇô49; contents 1935ÔÇô41).┬áA spokesperson for the gallery told Hyperallergic that additional details regarding the included artworks and the format of the new gallery space would become available closer to the exhibitionÔÇÖs opening date. 
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        New York
                      
                      
                        Marcel Duchamp
                      
                      
                        Gagosian Gallery

schema.headline: Gagosian to Debut New Gallery With DuchampÔÇÖs ÔÇťReadymadesÔÇŁ

title: Celeste Dupuy-Spencer, Artist Who Confronted Injustice, Dies at 46
description:
News
								She stood up for human rights in both her paintings and her advocacy, criticizing IsraelÔÇÖs violence against Palestinians and
body:
News
								She stood up for human rights in both her paintings and her advocacy, criticizing IsraelÔÇÖs violence against Palestinians and the rise of fascism in the US.
						
								
																
														
								
						
		
	  		    
        
            Celeste Dupuy-Spencer (photo by Stefanie Keenan/Getty Images)
    
		
				
						Celeste Dupuy-Spencer, a painter who confronted injustice at home and abroad with striking scenes of resistance, died on Friday, April 10, at the age of 46 at her home in Los Angeles, days before the opening of a new solo exhibition of her work at Jeffrey DeitchÔÇÖs West Hollywood gallery. The cause of her death was not publicly reported, and the gallery is expected to make an announcement about her show and a memorial in the coming days.Deitch, whose gallery announced the news of her death on Saturday, said Dupuy-Spencer was ÔÇťbeloved by people in her creative community.ÔÇŁ┬áÔÇťCeleste was an extraordinary artist and a wonderful person, deeply dedicated to her painting, often working round the clock in her studio,ÔÇŁ Deitch wrote in an email to Hyperallergic. ÔÇťWe spent many hours in her studio talking about the history of art and how it intersects with world events.ÔÇŁCeleste Dupuy-Spencer, "Father, DonÔÇÖt You See That I Am Burning" (2021) (photo Dawn Blackman, courtesy the artist)The California-based artist deconstructed violent moments of contemporary American politics in her work. Her vivid depiction of the January 6 insurrection, ÔÇťFather, DonÔÇÖt You See That I Am BurningÔÇŁ (2021), was acquired by the Hirshhorn Museum and Sculpture Garden in Washington, DC, in 2022.Dupry-Spencer also spoke in opposition to the atrocities Israel committed against Palestinians in Gaza and the Occupied West Bank. She was the target of relentless pro-Israel online harassment from the art world and beyond, but she continued to defend her stance in solidarity with the human rights of Palestinians.ÔÇťIÔÇÖm proud to be included on the list of people who stopped everything to cry out for an end to the genocide and a free Palestine,ÔÇŁ she posted on her Instagram page in September. ÔÇťI donÔÇÖt care what they do to me. Why would I duck and hide from fascists?ÔÇŁ┬á"To call a Jewish person a token for speaking against the state ÔÇö that is anti-Semitism," she wrote.Celeste Dupuy-Spencer, "The Alchemist, the Gospel, the Pillar of Fire (The Showcase)" (2024) (photo Charles White - JW Pictures, courtesy the artist and Jeffrey Deitch, New York and Los Angeles)Born in 1979, Dupry-Spencer grew up in Rheinbeck, New York, and traveled frequently to Louisiana before pursuing a Bachelor's of Fine Arts at Bard College in 2007 without graduating. She moved from New York to New Orleans in 2012 but eventually settled in Los Angeles, showing at Nino Mier Gallery and Eve FowlerÔÇÖs Artist Curated Projects.A decade after college, Dupry-Spencer found acclaim when her paintings were featured in the 2017 Whitney Biennial and the Hammer MuseumÔÇÖs 2018 Made in LA biennial. She reportedly contemplated shelving her art career while in New Orleans, but the experience of living in the Deep South inspired her to explore the origins of American mythologies and their reach into the present.ÔÇťOur relationship to that stuff is so twistedÔÇŽItÔÇÖs...desperately trying to be like, ÔÇśthat has nothing to do with meÔÇÖ ÔÇö when in fact it has everything to do with you,ÔÇŁ she told Pelican BombÔÇÖs Hyunjee Nicole Kim after refusing to attend a familyÔÇÖs wedding at a historic plantation.Celeste Dupuy-Spencer, "Veterans Day" (2017) (courtesy the artist and Whitney Museum of American Art)In 2021, Dupuy-Spencer became transfixed following Democracy Now! and CNN reports of MAGA activists ransacking the US Capitol as Congress certified the 2020 election. She first resisted painting the scene, but chose to depict the chaotic images of QAnoners, Proud Boys, and other camo-clad rioters with references to Caravaggio, Thomas Cole, and James EnsorÔÇÖs ÔÇťChristÔÇÖs Entry into Brussels in 1889ÔÇŁ (1888) and TintorettoÔÇÖs ÔÇťMoses Drawing Water from the RockÔÇŁ (1577).┬áAs she became more well-known, Dupuy-Spencer began speaking about her identity as a trans,┬ámasculine-presenting person while never seeing herself as fully male or female. (Dupuy-Spencer did not like using specific pronouns, but went by she/her in interviews.)ÔÇťI donÔÇÖt consider myself transitioning to male, but I was starting to do that and found myself reacquainted with something that I really love in myself ÔÇö the feminine side that was in a constant state of suppression,ÔÇŁ she told Los Angeles Magazine in a 2021 profile. ÔÇťBeing trans allowed me to understand what femininity really was.ÔÇŁCeleste Dupuy-Spencer, "Through the Laying of the Hands (Positively Demonic Dynamism)" (2018) (photo Lee Thompson/Flying Studio, courtesy the artist and Adam Green Art Advisory)Her upcoming solo show at Deitch, Burning in the Eyes of the Maker, grappled with the tension between the transactional nature of the art world and the political meanings she imbued in her works. In an artist statement for the exhibition, Dupuy-Spencer wrote that she approached her new paintings as a ÔÇťconversation rather than a constructionÔÇŁ and that the process of making work helped her reach inaccessible emotions and ideas.ÔÇťPainting, for me, is participation in the aliveness of life,ÔÇŁ she wrote. ÔÇťIt is communication as recognition, not explanation. Meaning emerges only through encounter ÔÇö when a viewer and a painting agree to stand in the same place and face the same uncertainty. The task is contact.ÔÇŁNina MacLaughlin, who co-authored a book with Dupuy-Spencer about her paintings that will be out with PhaidonÔÇÖs Monacelli Press this summer, called her loss ÔÇťa massive sadnessÔÇŁ and said working with her was ÔÇťone of the great honors of my life.ÔÇŁÔÇťCeleste saw beyond. She saw through and she saw beyond and she saw what was right here and she showed us. She showed what most of us try to ignore, deny, scroll past. She looked for us. There was deep tenderness in her rage,ÔÇŁ MacLaughlin told Hyperallergic. ÔÇťNo one had a mind like hers. No one. No one. She burned at a different temperature.ÔÇŁ
									
											
												News
											
											
												Obituaries
											
											
												Celeste Dupuy-Spencer
											
											
												California
											
											
												Los Angeles

schema.headline: Celeste Dupuy-Spencer, Artist Who Confronted Injustice, Dies at 46

title: Chicana Painter Criselda Vasquez Says ICE Detained Her Father
description:
News
                Vasquez was inspired by her Mexican immigrant parents to paint her celebrated 2017 portrait ÔÇťThe New Americ
body:
News
                Vasquez was inspired by her Mexican immigrant parents to paint her celebrated 2017 portrait ÔÇťThe New American Gothic.ÔÇŁ
            
                
                                
                            
                            
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Criselda Vasquez's parents posing in front of the artist's painting ÔÇťThe New American GothicÔÇŁ (2017) (photo courtesy the artist)Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has detained the father and portrait muse of California-based Chicana painter Criselda Vasquez, the artist said in an Instagram post. ÔÇťMy father and one of his workers were detained by ICE while simply on their way to work,ÔÇŁ Vasquez wrote on April 3. ÔÇťOur entire family is heartbroken, and my mother is completely devastated.ÔÇŁ The family has not publicly named Vasquez's father. Vasquez noted in her post that he had been in the United States for over 40 years now.In an April 13 email to Hyperallergic, Vasquez said that her father had been ÔÇťracially profiled on his way home from work, and┬átaken by Immigration authorities,ÔÇŁ on Tuesday, March 31, adding that he had been pulled over and arrested right outside of a neighbor's home.ÔÇťMy family and I have since made contact with him and know which detention facility he is being held in,ÔÇŁ Vasquez said. Through a GoFundMe linked on the artist's social media, the family raised nearly $68,000 of a $80,000 goal within 10 days of its launch. In a statement attached to the fundraiser, the family said the money would go toward his lost wages and legal fees associated with his detention.ÔÇťWhat I want the public to know about my father is that he is a devoted husband, parent, and grandfather, and the hardest-working, most selfless person I know,ÔÇŁ Vasquez continued in her email to Hyperallergic. ÔÇťHe has become a role model not only to his four children, all of whom are United States citizens, but also to many people he has come across.ÔÇŁÔÇťMy father's hard work, dedication, and sacrifices will never be forgotten and will continue to be a long lifetime inspiration to me and my family,ÔÇŁ Vasquez said.Criselda Vasquez, ÔÇťThe New American GothicÔÇŁ (2017) (Copyright ┬ę 2017 Criselda Vasquez; All rights reserved)Vasquez depicted her father in her celebrated 2017 painting ÔÇťThe New American GothicÔÇŁ (2017), a reimagining of Grant Wood's ÔÇťAmerican GothicÔÇŁ (1930) featuring both of her parents, originally from Mexico, holding cleaning products and a hoe. The Lucas Museum for Narrative Art in Los Angeles acquired the painting in 2021. In an earlier statement about the work, the artist had said that she sought to depict the ÔÇťplight and the plight of many in my community.ÔÇŁIn a previous social media post, Vasquez described her parents' influence on her figurative painting practice, which seeks to unveil hidden aspects of the Mexican-American life. ÔÇťWhen my parents pose for these paintings, their faces are reduced to extremely raw and somehow vulnerable expressions,ÔÇŁ Vasquez said. ÔÇťSadly, they strive to be invisible every day. They have dealt with constant rejection, suspicion, and fear so long, that it seems now that it comes naturally to them.ÔÇŁÔÇťIn the long tradition of immigrants that come to the United States, they have made homes here, and they are just trying to live a simple life with a bit of security and hopefulness for their children,ÔÇŁ Vasquez continued.The news of Vasquez's father's arrest comes amid President Trump's ongoing crackdown on immigrants in the United States. A recent report from the Deportation Data Project, which analyzes immigration enforcement data, outlined that ICE street arrests ÔÇö as in those occurring in neighborhoods, immigration courts, and check-in appointments at ICE offices ÔÇö had increased more than elevenfold during the first year of Trump's second presidency. It's also significantly harder to get released from immigration detention amid deportation proceedings, the report notes. 
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE)
                      
                      
                        Criselda Vasquez
                      
                      
                        California
                      
                      
                        Immigration
                      
                      
                        Trump Administration
